verses.commentary: Pharisees and teachers from Jerusalem gather. Official delegation. The religious establishment investigates.
They see disciples eating with unwashed (defiled) hands. Ritual violation observed. Not hygiene but ceremonial purity at stake.
Mark explains for Gentile readers: Pharisees and all Jews practice ceremonial washing, holding to the tradition of the elders. Oral law, not written Torah.
Washing after the marketplace and other traditions: cups, pitchers, kettles. Layers of human regulation added to divine command.
Why don't your disciples live according to tradition? Accusation framed as question. Tradition elevated to divine authority.
Jesus quotes Isaiah 29:13. You hypocrites. These people honor me with lips, but hearts are far from me. External conformity masks internal rebellion. Form without substance.
They worship in vain. Their teachings are human rules. Empty worship. God rejects religion that substitutes human tradition for divine command.
You let go of God's commands and hold on to human traditions. The exchange: divine for human. They invert priorities.
You have a fine way of setting aside God's commands to observe your traditions. Sarcasm. Their system is sophisticated evasion.
Moses said: Honor your father and mother (Exodus 20:12). Anyone who curses them is to be put to death (Exodus 21:17). Clear divine command. Sacred obligation.
But you say if anything is Corban (dedicated to God), it cannot help parents. Loophole. Declare resources as temple gifts, avoid parental support.
Then you no longer let them do anything for parents. Permission to disobey. Tradition overrides the fifth commandment.
You nullify God's word by your tradition. And you do many things like that. Systematic problem. Tradition becomes a weapon against Scripture.
Jesus calls the crowd. Public teaching. Listen to me, everyone, and understand. Universal address. Revolutionary statement coming.
Nothing outside a person defiles them. What comes out of a person defiles. Radical redefinition of purity. Moral, not ceremonial. Internal, not external. Levitical system redefined.
Verse missing in many manuscripts. Likely scribal insertion: If anyone has ears to hear, let them hear.
Disciples ask about this parable privately. They are confused. Even insiders struggle with Jesus' radical teaching.
Are you so dull? Rebuke. Don't you see that nothing entering from outside defiles? Physical food does not corrupt the soul.

Food goes into the stomach, then out of the body. Biological process, not spiritual corruption. Mark adds editorial comment: In saying this, Jesus declared all foods clean. Dietary laws abolished. Peter will struggle with this (Acts 10). Paul will build on it (Romans 14, Colossians 2:16).
What comes out of a person defiles them. The source is internal. Sin originates in the heart.
From within, out of the heart, come evil thoughts. The heart is the command center. Sexual immorality, theft, murder. The catalog begins.
Adultery, greed, malice, deceit, lewdness, envy, slander, arrogance, folly. Twelve vices. Comprehensive list. These come from within. Jeremiah 17:9: The heart is deceitful above all things.
All these evils come from inside and defile a person. The problem is not external contamination but internal corruption. Purity requires heart transformation, not ritual performance.
Jesus goes to Tyre, Gentile territory. He enters a house, wants secrecy. Impossible. His fame spreads beyond borders.
A woman with a demon-possessed daughter hears, comes, falls at his feet. Desperate mother.
She is Greek, born in Syrian Phoenicia. Gentile, pagan. She begs Jesus to drive out the demon. Faith crosses ethnic boundaries.

First let the children eat all they want. It is not right to take the children's bread and toss it to dogs. Shocking statement. Children = Jews. Dogs = Gentiles. Priority established. Jesus came first to Israel. But the harsh language tests her faith.

Lord, even the dogs under the table eat the children's crumbs. Brilliant response. She accepts her position, but appeals to overflow. She doesn't demand equality; she requests mercy. Her humility and wit demonstrate faith.
For such a reply, you may go. The demon has left your daughter. Her faith wins. Jesus grants her request. Word spoken, daughter healed at a distance.
She goes home, finds the child on the bed, demon gone. Healing confirmed. Faith vindicated.
Jesus travels through Tyre, Sidon, down to the Sea of Galilee, into the Decapolis. Extended Gentile itinerary. The kingdom expands geographically.
People bring a deaf man with a speech impediment. They beg Jesus to touch him. Faith mediated by friends.
Jesus takes him aside, away from the crowd. Privacy. He puts fingers in the man's ears, spits, touches his tongue. Physical actions. Incarnational ministry. Touch conveys grace.

Jesus looks to heaven, sighs deeply, says Ephphatha (Aramaic: Be opened). The sigh reveals the weight of living in a fallen world. Healing costs something. The command is simple; the compassion is profound.
Ears opened, tongue loosened, he speaks plainly. Complete restoration. Physical and social healing. He can now hear God's word and speak God's praise.
Jesus commands silence. But the more he commanded, the more they proclaimed. Irony. The messianic secret cannot be kept. Good news must be shared.

People overwhelmed with amazement. He has done everything well. Echo of Genesis 1:31: God saw all he had made, and it was very good. Jesus is the new creation. He makes the deaf hear and mute speak. Isaiah 35:5–6 fulfilled: messianic signs. Chapter seven: Jesus redefines purity (internal, not external), extends mercy to Gentiles, and heals in Gentile territory. The kingdom breaks every boundary.
